title: Tuples
description: pair, generalized beyond two elements: bundling any fixed number of values with get<index>() and tie() for unpacking.
objectives: Understand tuple as pair generalized to more than two elements
Create a tuple and access elements with get<index>()
Unpack a tuple into separate variables with tie()
Know when tuple is preferable to pair

sections.text: pair bundles exactly two values. What about three, four, or more related values that belong together? That's what tuple, from <tuple>, is for.
tuple<T1, T2, T3, ...> bundles any fixed number of values, possibly of different types, into a single object.

Unlike pair's named .first/.second, tuple elements are accessed by position using get<0>(student), get<1>(student), and so on — zero-indexed, exactly like the arrays and vectors we've used throughout. There's no .first for a tuple; the index must be known at compile time (it's inside <>, not ()) since each position can hold a different type.

tie() takes existing variables and assigns each element of the tuple into the matching one, all in a single line — a convenient way to "unpack" a tuple's contents without writing get<0>(), get<1>(), get<2>() separately.
When exactly two values are needed, pair is generally preferred for its clearer .first/.second names. tuple is reached for when three or more values genuinely need to travel together.

sections.title: A luggage tag with three fields instead of two
sections.text: Recall pair's luggage tag with a name and destination. A tuple is a luggage tag with more fields stapled on — name, destination, AND flight number — still one tag, just holding more glued-together information, referenced by which field number it is rather than a name.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to use .first/.second on a tuple — that syntax is specific to pair; tuple uses get<index>().
Using a runtime variable as the index inside get<>() — the index must be a compile-time constant, since each position can be a different type.
Reaching for tuple when pair would do — for exactly two values, pair's named members are usually clearer.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a tuple, and how does it relate to pair?
questions.answer: tuple bundles any fixed number of values of possibly different types into one object — a generalization of pair, which is limited to exactly two values.

questions.question: How do you access an element of a tuple?
questions.answer: By position, using get<index>(t), with the index starting at 0 and required to be a compile-time constant.

questions.question: What does tie() do with a tuple?
questions.answer: It unpacks a tuple's elements directly into a set of existing variables in one line, instead of retrieving each with get<index>() separately.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: tuple<T1, T2, T3, ...> generalizes pair beyond two elements, bundling any fixed number of values of possibly different types. Elements are accessed by position with get<index>(t), zero-indexed and requiring a compile-time constant index, rather than pair's named .first/.second. tie(a, b, c) = t; is a convenient way to unpack all of a tuple's elements into existing variables at once. pair remains preferable for exactly two values, thanks to its clearer named members; tuple is reached for when three or more related values need to travel together.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Tuples
sections.text: tuple = a luggage tag with more than two fields, referenced by number: get<0>(), get<1>(), get<2>(). tie() unpacks them all at once.
